text.title: django-decision-tree Core Rules
text.summary: Six testable rules for an architectural decision record at Django bootstrap or refactor checkpoint.

rule.statement: Score the project on five signals: needs admin? needs ORM? needs server-rendered pages? needs user accounts? needs server-side forms? Score ≥ 4 → Django. Score 2–3 → consider Django Ninja (Django ORM + FastAPI-like API). Score ≤ 1 → FastAPI or Flask.
rule.rationale: Django's batteries-included surface is leverage on full web apps; pure overhead on API-only services. The signal score correlates with Django's break-even point empirically.

rule.statement: Pick DRF when: existing DRF code, complex permission hierarchies, drf-spectacular OpenAPI required, team familiarity. Pick Django Ninja when: greenfield, async-first, Pydantic v2 validation desired, FastAPI-like DX. Never mix DRF + Ninja in the same Django app.
rule.rationale: URL routing, auth context, OpenAPI generation diverge. Mixing produces inconsistent client SDKs and doubles failure surface.

rule.statement: Pick layering tier from model count and team size: ≤ 10 models + 1–2 devs → "simple" (fat models). 10–50 models + 3–10 devs → "service-layer" (selectors / services / commands). ≥ 50 models + 10+ devs OR multiple bounded contexts → "clean-arch" (DDD + repositories).
rule.rationale: Simple architecture is the right cost for an MVP; clean-arch is the right cost for an enterprise codebase. Mismatch produces either rewrites or under-architected pain.

rule.statement: Pick PostgreSQL by default. Pick SQLite for dev/test + truly single-server side projects (no replication, low write concurrency). Pick MySQL only when existing infra forces it. Pick a managed Postgres (RDS / Cloud SQL / Neon) for production whenever possible.
rule.rationale: PostgreSQL has the best Django integration (JSONB, full-text search, partial indexes, generated columns). SQLite is fine for dev but cannot scale concurrent writes. MySQL trails on advanced features.

rule.statement: Pick deployment target from team DevOps maturity + scale: low maturity + low scale → PaaS (Render / Fly.io / Railway). Medium maturity + cost focus → VPS (Hetzner / DigitalOcean) with systemd + nginx. High maturity + need for horizontal scaling → Kubernetes. Highly variable traffic + stateless → Serverless (Lambda / Cloud Run).
rule.rationale: Picking K8s without operators wastes 6+ months of "we'll learn it". Picking PaaS at million-req/s wastes margin. Match deployment to operational capacity, not aspirational.

rule.statement: For every candidate third-party package, audit eight signals: commit within 6 months, Django 5.x + Python 3.11+ compat, ≥ 1k stars OR clear maintainer track-record, complete docs, CI green, no known CVEs, licence compatible, cannot-be-done-with-builtins=true. Reject "high-risk" (≤ 1k stars + no recent commits) unless a sunset plan exists.
rule.rationale: Unmaintained packages are the dominant source of long-tail security issues and refactor cost. Eight signals catch most low-effort risks.
